6. Matrix algebra for regression

MATH 4210, Chapter 6

Español

Dwaine Studios runs a chain of portrait studios and wants to grow. It already operates in 21 cities, and its managers track two numbers for each: the population aged 16 and younger, the pool of children whose parents buy portraits, and per-capita disposable income, how much money families have to spend. They want to use these two numbers to predict studio sales, so they can rank new cities and open where the forecast is highest. The question is a forecasting one, but underneath it is a modeling one: how do sales depend on both predictors at once?

In Chapter 2 you fit a line with a single predictor by solving two normal equations for the intercept and slope (2.2 Least squares from first principles). That worked because there were only two unknowns. Dwaine has three, one intercept and two slopes, and a realistic study might have twenty. Writing out a separate normal equation for each and solving the tangle by hand is not something anyone wants to do twice. Matrix algebra is the better language: it packs a whole dataset into two symbols, the normal equations into one, and the solution into a formula you write in a single line and compute in a single command.

None of what follows requires you to have seen a matrix before. We build every idea from its definition, test it on a small example you could check with a pencil, then run it on the real 21-city dataset in both R and Python. If you have taken linear algebra, this will read like review with a statistical accent; if not, you will still finish able to fit and explain a multiple regression, the only matrix algebra this course asks of you.

Figure 1 shows that both predictors matter: sales climb as the young population grows, and for a given population the higher-income cities (darker points) tend to sell more. A model that uses both at once is a multiple regression model, and every such model is built, fit, and understood through matrices. By the end you will have built, for the real Dwaine data, the design matrix X\mathbf{X}X, the product X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X, its inverse, the coefficient estimates, the hat matrix, and every coefficient’s standard error, each from operations you can also do by hand on a small example.

This lesson at a glance

  • What we are doing: Learning just enough matrix algebra to write and solve a regression with several predictors at once, using the 21-city Dwaine Studios data as the running example.

  • Why we are doing it: Chapter 2’s two normal equations (2.2 Least squares from first principles) worked for one predictor, but Dwaine has two and a real study may have twenty; solving them predictor by predictor by hand is hopeless, so we need a compact language.

  • Main objective: Represent the data as X\mathbf{X}X and Y\mathbf{Y}Y, form X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X and X′Y\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}X′Y, solve b=(X′X)−1X′Y\mathbf{b} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}b=(X′X)−1X′Y, build the hat matrix, and read each coefficient’s variance off σ2(X′X)−1\sigma^2(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}σ2(X′X)−1, checking every step on a small example first.

  • What changed from the last chapters: Until now, one predictor and scalar algebra; from here, many predictors packed into vectors and matrices, still the same least-squares idea seen through a new notation. The exact Dwaine X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X built here is the object Chapter 7 reuses (7.1 The model and least squares in matrix form).

Learning objectives

By the end of this chapter you will be able to:

  • Represent a regression dataset as a response vector Y\mathbf{Y}Y and a design matrix X\mathbf{X}X, and write the model as Y=Xβ+ε\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}Y=Xβ+ε.

  • Compute transposes and matrix products, and in particular build X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X and X′Y\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}X′Y, by hand and with software.

  • Decide when a square matrix has an inverse, using rank and the determinant, and explain what perfect collinearity does to X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X.

  • Solve the normal equations X′X b=X′Y\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}\,\mathbf{b} = \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}X′Xb=X′Y for the coefficient vector b=(X′X)−1X′Y\mathbf{b} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}b=(X′X)−1X′Y.

  • Verify that the hat matrix H=X(X′X)−1X′\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'H=X(X′X)−1X′ is symmetric and idempotent, and explain why fitted values and residuals are projections.

  • Express SSE, SSR, and SSTO as quadratic forms, and connect positive definiteness to nonnegative sums of squares.

  • Derive the mean vector and covariance matrix of a random vector, and use them to show Cov⁡{b}=σ2(X′X)−1\operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{b}\} = \sigma^2 (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}Cov{b}=σ2(X′X)−1.

  • State the multivariate normal model for Y\mathbf{Y}Y and b\mathbf{b}b and describe how it powers the inference of later chapters.

6.1 The data as a vector and a matrix¶

Intuition¶

Start with the response. Dwaine has 21 sales figures, one per city. Stack them into a single column and you have a vector (Definition 6.1), an ordered list of numbers written vertically. Call it Y\mathbf{Y}Y. A vector is the simplest matrix: many rows, one column.

Now the predictors. Each city carries two numbers, so the predictors form a table with 21 rows and 2 columns, and a table of numbers with rows and columns is a matrix (Definition 6.1). For regression we glue one extra column of all ones onto the front: a bookkeeping trick that lets the intercept ride along as just another coefficient. The result is the design matrix X\mathbf{X}X (Definition 6.2), with 21 rows and 3 columns. Figure 2 shows how the whole model lines up as stacked arrays.

Reading across a row gives one city’s complete record: a 1, its young population, its disposable income, and, in Y\mathbf{Y}Y, its sales. Stacking the rows turns 21 separate little equations into the single statement Y=Xβ+ε\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}Y=Xβ+ε. That compression is the reason to learn matrices: the same three symbols describe a dataset with 21 rows or 21 million.

Formula¶

Definition 6.1: Matrix, vector, and dimension

A matrix is a rectangular array of numbers arranged in rows and columns. Its dimension is written (rows) ×\times× (columns), so X\mathbf{X}X is 21×321 \times 321×3, and the entry in row iii, column jjj is denoted XijX_{ij}Xij​. A vector is a matrix with a single column (a column vector) or a single row (a row vector).

For a regression with nnn observations and ppp parameters (counting the intercept), the pieces are

Y=(Y1Y2⋮Yn)n×1,X=(1X11⋯X1,p−11X21⋯X2,p−1⋮⋮⋮1Xn1⋯Xn,p−1)n×p,β=(β0β1⋮βp−1)p×1.\mathbf{Y} = \begin{pmatrix} Y_1 \\ Y_2 \\ \vdots \\ Y_n \end{pmatrix}_{n \times 1}, \qquad \mathbf{X} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & X_{11} & \cdots & X_{1,p-1} \\ 1 & X_{21} & \cdots & X_{2,p-1} \\ \vdots & \vdots & & \vdots \\ 1 & X_{n1} & \cdots & X_{n,p-1} \end{pmatrix}_{n \times p}, \qquad \boldsymbol{\beta} = \begin{pmatrix} \beta_0 \\ \beta_1 \\ \vdots \\ \beta_{p-1} \end{pmatrix}_{p \times 1}.Y=⎝⎛​Y1​Y2​⋮Yn​​⎠⎞​n×1​,X=⎝⎛​11⋮1​X11​X21​⋮Xn1​​⋯⋯⋯​X1,p−1​X2,p−1​⋮Xn,p−1​​⎠⎞​n×p​,β=⎝⎛​β0​β1​⋮βp−1​​⎠⎞​p×1​.

  • Y\mathbf{Y}Y is the response vector; row iii is the response for case iii.

  • X\mathbf{X}X is the design matrix; its first column is all ones, and column k+1k+1k+1 holds the values of predictor kkk.

  • β\boldsymbol{\beta}β is the parameter vector: the intercept β0\beta_0β0​ followed by the slopes.

  • ppp is the number of regression parameters including the intercept (p=3p = 3p=3 for Dwaine), and nnn is the number of observations (n=21n = 21n=21).

Definition 6.2: Design matrix

The design matrix X\mathbf{X}X is the n×pn \times pn×p matrix whose first column is all ones, carrying the intercept, and whose remaining p−1p - 1p−1 columns hold the predictor values, one row per observation. With the response vector Y\mathbf{Y}Y and parameter vector β\boldsymbol{\beta}β it packs the whole model into Y=Xβ+ε\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}Y=Xβ+ε.

For Dwaine, n=21n = 21n=21 and p=3p = 3p=3: one column of ones, one column of under-16 populations, one column of disposable incomes. The whole model of Chapter 2 generalizes to Y=Xβ+ε\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}Y=Xβ+ε, which we unpack in 7.1 The model and least squares in matrix form. In words: each observed response equals a weighted sum of that row’s predictors, using the same weights β\boldsymbol{\beta}β for every row, plus a random error.

R and Python¶

Example 6.1: Building the Dwaine design matrix

Question. Read the Dwaine data and assemble the response vector Y\mathbf{Y}Y and the design matrix X\mathbf{X}X, with its leading column of ones.

Intuition. The response is just the sales column. The design matrix is the two predictor columns with a column of ones bolted onto the left.

Formula. X\mathbf{X}X is n×p=21×3n \times p = 21 \times 3n×p=21×3; column 1 is ones, column 2 is targtpop, column 3 is dispoinc.

Computation.

dwaine <- read.csv("data/dwaine.csv")
dim(dwaine)
head(dwaine, 3)
[1] 21  3
  targtpop dispoinc sales
1     68.5     16.7 174.4
2     45.2     16.8 164.4
3     91.3     18.2 244.2
n <- nrow(dwaine)
X <- cbind(1, dwaine$targtpop, dwaine$dispoinc)
colnames(X) <- c("intercept", "targtpop", "dispoinc")
Y <- dwaine$sales
head(X, 3)
     intercept targtpop dispoinc
[1,]         1     68.5     16.7
[2,]         1     45.2     16.8
[3,]         1     91.3     18.2

Now in Python, with numpy assembling the same array from the same CSV.

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf

dwaine = pd.read_csv("data/dwaine.csv")
print(dwaine.shape)
print(dwaine.head(3))
(21, 3)
   targtpop  dispoinc  sales
0      68.5      16.7  174.4
1      45.2      16.8  164.4
2      91.3      18.2  244.2
n = len(dwaine)
X = np.column_stack([np.ones(n), dwaine["targtpop"], dwaine["dispoinc"]])
Y = dwaine["sales"].to_numpy()
print(X[:3])
[[ 1.  68.5 16.7]
 [ 1.  45.2 16.8]
 [ 1.  91.3 18.2]]

Interpretation. The first city has 68,500 young residents and 16,700per−capitaincome,andtheleading16,700 per-capita income, and the leading 16,700per−capitaincome,andtheleading1istheplaceholderthatwillmultiplytheintercept.Everyrowof is the placeholder that will multiply the intercept. Every row of istheplaceholderthatwillmultiplytheintercept.Everyrowof\mathbf{X}$ reads “one, then this city’s predictors.” R’s cbind and Python’s np.column_stack built the same array from the same file, so the two languages share one object, and the whole fit becomes a short sequence of matrix operations.

Try it 6.1

A regression uses one intercept and four predictors on n=30n = 30n=30 observations. Give the dimensions of Y\mathbf{Y}Y, X\mathbf{X}X, and β\boldsymbol{\beta}β, and say what the very first column of X\mathbf{X}X contains.

Solution

Here n=30n = 30n=30 and p=5p = 5p=5 (four slopes plus the intercept). So Y\mathbf{Y}Y is 30×130 \times 130×1, X\mathbf{X}X is 30×530 \times 530×5, and β\boldsymbol{\beta}β is 5×15 \times 15×1. The first column of X\mathbf{X}X is all ones, the placeholder that carries the intercept β0\beta_0β0​.

6.2 Transpose and matrix multiplication¶

Intuition¶

We have the data in matrices; now we need to combine them. Two operations do almost all the work. The transpose (Definition 6.3) flips a matrix on its diagonal so rows become columns. Matrix multiplication (Definition 6.4) is the engine that turns the design matrix into the sums of squares and cross-products a regression runs on.

Matrix multiplication is not what a newcomer expects. One entry of the product is built from a whole row of the left matrix and a whole column of the right: line them up, multiply term by term, and add. Applied to X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X, that rule produces exactly the sums ∑XikXij\sum X_{ik} X_{ij}∑Xik​Xij​ you would otherwise compute one painful sum at a time. Figure 3 shows it on a small example.

A common wrong turn

A frequent first mistake is to think X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X means “square each entry of X\mathbf{X}X,” or that multiplying two matrices multiplies matching cells. Neither is true. Matrix multiplication mixes a full row with a full column. Because of that, order matters: AB\mathbf{A}\mathbf{B}AB and BA\mathbf{B}\mathbf{A}BA are usually different, and often only one of them is even defined.

Two products, and only two, matter for the fit: X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X summarizes the predictors by themselves, and X′Y\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}X′Y summarizes how each predictor moves with the response. Between them they hold every summary least squares needs, which is why a whole dataset can shrink to a handful of numbers: the fit does not care about individual rows once these sums are known.

Key idea

A regression sees the data only through the two products X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X and X′Y\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}X′Y. Twenty-one cities or twenty-one million, once those sums are formed the individual rows are never needed again, and the entire fit is a fixed sequence of operations on those two small objects.

Formula¶

Definition 6.3: Transpose

The transpose of a matrix A\mathbf{A}A, written A′\mathbf{A}'A′, swaps rows and columns: the entry in row iii, column jjj of A′\mathbf{A}'A′ is the entry in row jjj, column iii of A\mathbf{A}A. If A\mathbf{A}A is m×km \times km×k, then A′\mathbf{A}'A′ is k×mk \times mk×m.

Picture it as tipping a matrix over its top-left corner: each row stands up to become a column, so a wide matrix tips into a tall one and back. A column vector, tipped over, becomes a single row, which is why u′u\mathbf{u}'\mathbf{u}u′u multiplies a lying-down copy of a vector by a standing-up copy and collapses to one number.

Definition 6.4: Matrix multiplication and conformability

Two matrices are conformable for multiplication when the number of columns of the left equals the number of rows of the right. If A\mathbf{A}A is m×km \times km×k and B\mathbf{B}B is k×qk \times qk×q, the product AB\mathbf{A}\mathbf{B}AB is m×qm \times qm×q, with entries

(AB)ij=∑ℓ=1kAiℓ Bℓj,(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{B})_{ij} = \sum_{\ell=1}^{k} A_{i\ell}\, B_{\ell j},(AB)ij​=ℓ=1∑k​Aiℓ​Bℓj​,

This row-by-column rule is matrix multiplication: entry (i,j)(i,j)(i,j) is row iii of A\mathbf{A}A multiplied with column jjj of B\mathbf{B}B, term by term and summed.

  • The inner dimensions (kkk and kkk) must match; they are what you sum over.

  • The outer dimensions (mmm and qqq) give the shape of the answer.

  • Entry (i,j)(i,j)(i,j) is row iii of A\mathbf{A}A dotted with column jjj of B\mathbf{B}B.

Two products carry the whole load in regression. With X\mathbf{X}X of size n×pn \times pn×p, the transpose X′\mathbf{X}'X′ is p×np \times np×n, so

X′X is p×p,X′Y is p×1.\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X} \ \text{is}\ p \times p, \qquad \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}\ \text{is}\ p \times 1.X′X is p×p,X′Y is p×1.

In words: X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X collects every sum of squares and cross-product of the predictor columns into a small square table, and X′Y\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}X′Y collects every predictor-times-response sum into a short column, the raw materials of the normal equations. A rule we will use constantly is that transposing a product reverses the order of its factors.

Theorem 6.5: Transpose of a product

For any conformable matrices A\mathbf{A}A and B\mathbf{B}B,

(AB)′=B′A′.(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{B})' = \mathbf{B}'\mathbf{A}'.(AB)′=B′A′.

Proof. We show (AB)′=B′A′(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{B})' = \mathbf{B}'\mathbf{A}'(AB)′=B′A′ by comparing entries. The (i,j)(i,j)(i,j) entry of (AB)′(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{B})'(AB)′ is, by the definition of transpose, the (j,i)(j,i)(j,i) entry of AB\mathbf{A}\mathbf{B}AB, which is ∑ℓAjℓBℓi\sum_{\ell} A_{j\ell} B_{\ell i}∑ℓ​Ajℓ​Bℓi​. The (i,j)(i,j)(i,j) entry of B′A′\mathbf{B}'\mathbf{A}'B′A′ is row iii of B′\mathbf{B}'B′ dotted with column jjj of A′\mathbf{A}'A′, that is ∑ℓ(B′)iℓ(A′)ℓj=∑ℓBℓiAjℓ\sum_{\ell} (\mathbf{B}')_{i\ell} (\mathbf{A}')_{\ell j} = \sum_{\ell} B_{\ell i} A_{j \ell}∑ℓ​(B′)iℓ​(A′)ℓj​=∑ℓ​Bℓi​Ajℓ​. The two sums have the same terms, so the matrices are equal. ■\blacksquare■

R and Python¶

First the small example from Figure 3, so the mechanics are concrete before we turn the engine loose on the real data. In R, %*% is matrix multiplication (a plain * multiplies entry by entry instead); in Python the operator is @.

A <- matrix(c(1, 2, 3,
              4, 5, 6), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE)
B <- matrix(c(1, 0,
              0, 1,
              1, 1), nrow = 3, byrow = TRUE)
A %*% B
     [,1] [,2]
[1,]    4    5
[2,]   10   11
A = np.array([[1, 2, 3],
              [4, 5, 6]])
B = np.array([[1, 0],
              [0, 1],
              [1, 1]])
print(A @ B)
[[ 4  5]
 [10 11]]
Example 6.2: The matrices X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X and X′Y\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}X′Y for Dwaine

Question. Form X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X and X′Y\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}X′Y for the Dwaine data. What do the individual entries mean?

Intuition. X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X is a 3×33 \times 33×3 table whose entries are sums of products of the predictor columns; X′Y\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}X′Y is a length-3 column pairing each predictor column with sales.

Formula. (X′X)jk=∑iXijXik(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})_{jk} = \sum_i X_{ij} X_{ik}(X′X)jk​=∑i​Xij​Xik​ and (X′Y)j=∑iXijYi(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y})_j = \sum_i X_{ij} Y_i(X′Y)j​=∑i​Xij​Yi​.

Computation.

XtX <- t(X) %*% X
XtX
          intercept targtpop dispoinc
intercept      21.0  1302.40   360.00
targtpop     1302.4 87707.94 22609.19
dispoinc      360.0 22609.19  6190.26
XtY <- t(X) %*% Y
XtY
               [,1]
intercept   3820.00
targtpop  249643.35
dispoinc   66072.75
XtX = X.T @ X
print(XtX)
[[2.100000e+01 1.302400e+03 3.600000e+02]
 [1.302400e+03 8.770794e+04 2.260919e+04]
 [3.600000e+02 2.260919e+04 6.190260e+03]]
XtY = X.T @ Y
print(XtY)
[  3820.   249643.35  66072.75]

Interpretation. The top-left entry of X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X is 21=∑1⋅1=n21 = \sum 1 \cdot 1 = n21=∑1⋅1=n, the sample size. The rest of the first row and column are the predictor sums ∑targtpop=1302.4\sum \text{targtpop} = 1302.4∑targtpop=1302.4 and ∑dispoinc=360.0\sum \text{dispoinc} = 360.0∑dispoinc=360.0. The diagonal entries 87707.94 and 6190.26 are the sums of squared predictor values, and the off-diagonal 22609.19 is the cross-product ∑(targtpop)(dispoinc)\sum (\text{targtpop})(\text{dispoinc})∑(targtpop)(dispoinc). In X′Y\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}X′Y, the first entry 3820 is ∑Y\sum Y∑Y, and the other two are ∑(targtpop)Y\sum(\text{targtpop})Y∑(targtpop)Y and ∑(dispoinc)Y\sum(\text{dispoinc})Y∑(dispoinc)Y. Chapter 7 reuses this exact X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X (7.1 The model and least squares in matrix form).

Try it 6.2

Let u=(1,2,3)′\mathbf{u} = (1, 2, 3)'u=(1,2,3)′ be a 3×13 \times 13×1 column vector. Without software, compute u′u\mathbf{u}'\mathbf{u}u′u and say in words what it equals. Then give the dimension of uu′\mathbf{u}\mathbf{u}'uu′ (you need not compute all its entries).

Solution

u′u\mathbf{u}'\mathbf{u}u′u multiplies a 1×31 \times 31×3 row by a 3×13 \times 13×1 column, giving a 1×11 \times 11×1 number: 12+22+32=141^2 + 2^2 + 3^2 = 1412+22+32=14, the sum of squared entries. Reversing the order, uu′\mathbf{u}\mathbf{u}'uu′ multiplies a 3×13 \times 13×1 column by a 1×31 \times 31×3 row, giving a 3×33 \times 33×3 matrix. Same vectors, opposite order, different shapes: order matters in matrix multiplication.

Durable skill: Check the shapes before you trust the numbers

Every matrix expression has a shape, and the shapes must fit before any answer can be right. When you meet a formula like X′Y\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}X′Y, read off the dimensions first: X′\mathbf{X}'X′ is p×np \times np×n, Y\mathbf{Y}Y is n×1n \times 1n×1, the inner nnn’s match, so the result is p×1p \times 1p×1. If they did not match, the expression would be nonsense and you would stop before computing a number. This dimension check catches a large share of real matrix bugs in seconds; practice it by narrating the shape of every product you write.

The row-by-column rule is easier to trust once you have watched it happen on the one product a regression actually needs, so build X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X yourself before moving on.

6.3 Identity, symmetry, independence, and rank¶

Intuition¶

Before inverting anything, we need vocabulary for the shapes of matrices that show up in regression, and one idea that decides whether a fit is even possible. The identity matrix I\mathbf{I}I (Definition 6.6) is the matrix version of the number 1: multiplying by it changes nothing. A symmetric matrix (Definition 6.7) equals its own transpose, and X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X is always symmetric, which is why its table looks like a mirror across the diagonal.

The idea that decides feasibility is linear independence (Definition 6.8) of the predictor columns. If one predictor column is an exact linear combination of the others, say dispoinc were exactly twice targtpop plus a constant, then the columns carry redundant information, the matrix X\mathbf{X}X is rank deficient, and X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X cannot be inverted. There is then no unique best fit, because the data cannot tell the redundant coefficients apart. This is the matrix face of a problem you will meet again as multicollinearity in 12.1 Multicollinearity and the variance inflation factor.

A picture makes independence concrete. Think of two columns as arrows drawn from the origin. If they point in genuinely different directions, they open up an area between them, and the matrix has full rank. If one is just a stretched copy of the other, both arrows lie on a single line, the area between them is zero, and the matrix is rank deficient. Figure 5 shows both cases side by side.

These names earn their keep: a named property is a fact you can cite instead of recompute. Showing the hat matrix is a projection becomes a one-line argument, “it is symmetric and idempotent,” in place of pages of entry-by-entry checking.

Formula¶

Definition 6.6: Identity matrix

The identity matrix In\mathbf{I}_nIn​ is the n×nn \times nn×n matrix with ones on the diagonal and zeros elsewhere. For any conformable A\mathbf{A}A, IA=A\mathbf{I}\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{A}IA=A and AI=A\mathbf{A}\mathbf{I} = \mathbf{A}AI=A.

Definition 6.7: Symmetric matrix

A square matrix A\mathbf{A}A is symmetric if A′=A\mathbf{A}' = \mathbf{A}A′=A.

The product X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X is always symmetric, because (X′X)′=X′(X′)′=X′X(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})' = \mathbf{X}'(\mathbf{X}')' = \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}(X′X)′=X′(X′)′=X′X by the order-reversing rule (Theorem 6.5) of 6.2 Transpose and matrix multiplication.

Definition 6.8: Linear independence, rank, and full column rank

Columns v1,…,vp\mathbf{v}_1, \dots, \mathbf{v}_pv1​,…,vp​ are linearly independent if the only weights c1,…,cpc_1, \dots, c_pc1​,…,cp​ making

c1v1+c2v2+⋯+cpvp=0c_1 \mathbf{v}_1 + c_2 \mathbf{v}_2 + \cdots + c_p \mathbf{v}_p = \mathbf{0}c1​v1​+c2​v2​+⋯+cp​vp​=0

are all zero; that is, no column can be written using the others. The rank of a matrix is the number of linearly independent columns it has, and the design matrix X\mathbf{X}X has full column rank when its rank equals ppp.

The central fact of this section connects rank to invertibility.

Theorem 6.9: Invertibility and full column rank

The matrix X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X is invertible if and only if X\mathbf{X}X has full column rank. When it does, the least-squares fit is unique; when it does not, it is not.

A tiny example makes rank concrete: (1,0)′(1, 0)'(1,0)′ and (0,1)′(0, 1)'(0,1)′ are independent, but (1,2)′(1, 2)'(1,2)′ and (2,4)′(2, 4)'(2,4)′ are dependent because the second is exactly twice the first, so together they span only a line and have rank 1, not 2. In a design matrix, such a dependent column is a predictor that repeats information already present.

R and Python¶

Software reports symmetry, rank, and the determinant (the single number, covered in 6.4 The inverse and the normal equations, whose vanishing signals rank deficiency) directly.

isSymmetric(XtX)
qr(X)$rank
round(det(XtX), 2)
[1] TRUE
[1] 3
[1] 1068302
print(np.allclose(XtX, XtX.T))
print(np.linalg.matrix_rank(X))
print(round(np.linalg.det(XtX), 2))
True
3
1068302.4

The design matrix has rank 3, its full column count, so the two predictors plus the intercept carry three genuinely different pieces of information, and the nonzero determinant confirms that X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X is invertible and the Dwaine fit is well posed.

The habit to build: before trusting any multiple regression, ask whether the predictors are genuinely distinct. Software will happily invert a nearly singular matrix and return coefficients with enormous standard errors, the early warning that two predictors carry almost the same information. Rank is the yes-or-no version of that question; Chapter 12 sharpens it into “how close to dependent are they?”

Try it 6.3

Suppose someone adds a third predictor to the Dwaine model: targtpop measured in hundreds instead of thousands, that is, exactly 10×targtpop10 \times \text{targtpop}10×targtpop. What is the rank of the new 21×421 \times 421×4 design matrix, and can X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X be inverted? Explain.

Solution

The new column is exactly 10 times an existing one, so it adds no independent information. The rank stays at 3, not 4, so X\mathbf{X}X is rank deficient and X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X (now 4×44 \times 44×4) is singular and cannot be inverted: there is no unique least-squares solution, because the data cannot split one effect between two copies of the same predictor.

6.4 The inverse and the normal equations¶

Intuition¶

Here is where the machinery pays off. In ordinary algebra, to solve ax=cax = cax=c you divide by aaa, which is the same as multiplying by a−1a^{-1}a−1. Matrices have no division, but they have the next best thing: for a square matrix A\mathbf{A}A with full rank, there is an inverse A−1\mathbf{A}^{-1}A−1 (Definition 6.10) that undoes it, meaning A−1A=I\mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{I}A−1A=I. To solve a matrix equation Ax=c\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{c}Ax=c, multiply both sides on the left by A−1\mathbf{A}^{-1}A−1 and get x=A−1c\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{c}x=A−1c.

The normal equations of least squares, which in Chapter 2 were two scalar equations, collapse in matrix form to the single equation X′X b=X′Y\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}\,\mathbf{b} = \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}X′Xb=X′Y. Chapter 7 derives this from minimizing squared error (7.1 The model and least squares in matrix form); for now take it as the matrix twin of the Chapter 2 normal equations (2.2 Least squares from first principles). Solving it is now a one-liner: multiply by (X′X)−1(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}(X′X)−1.

With numbers, a−1a^{-1}a−1 exists for every aaa except zero. With matrices, A−1\mathbf{A}^{-1}A−1 exists for every square matrix except the rank-deficient ones, so “can I solve this uniquely,” “is the determinant nonzero,” and “are the columns independent” are three ways of asking one question. For Dwaine, all three say yes.

A common wrong turn

There is no such thing as “dividing by a matrix,” and you cannot cancel X′\mathbf{X}'X′ from both sides of X′Xb=X′Y\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}\mathbf{b} = \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}X′Xb=X′Y to get Xb=Y\mathbf{X}\mathbf{b} = \mathbf{Y}Xb=Y. That step would need (X′)−1(\mathbf{X}')^{-1}(X′)−1, but X′\mathbf{X}'X′ is p×np \times np×n and not even square, so it has no inverse. The only legal move is to invert the square matrix X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X.

Formula¶

Definition 6.10: Inverse and determinant

A square matrix A\mathbf{A}A is invertible (or nonsingular) if there is a matrix A−1\mathbf{A}^{-1}A−1 with

A−1A=A A−1=I.\mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{A}\,\mathbf{A}^{-1} = \mathbf{I}.A−1A=AA−1=I.

Such an inverse exists exactly when A\mathbf{A}A has full rank, equivalently when its determinant, a single number computed from the entries, is nonzero. For a 2×22 \times 22×2 matrix,

det⁡(abcd)=ad−bc,(abcd)−1=1ad−bc(d−b−ca).\det\begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix} = ad - bc, \qquad \begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix}^{-1} = \frac{1}{ad - bc}\begin{pmatrix} d & -b \\ -c & a \end{pmatrix}.det(ac​bd​)=ad−bc,(ac​bd​)−1=ad−bc1​(d−c​−ba​).

  • The determinant ad−bcad - bcad−bc measures whether the columns are independent; if it is zero, the inverse formula divides by zero and no inverse exists.

  • For larger matrices the determinant is more involved, so we let software compute it, but the rule “invertible if and only if determinant nonzero” stays exact.

This connects back to the picture of rank in Figure 5: the two columns of a two-by-two matrix frame a parallelogram, and the determinant is its area. When the columns point along the same line the parallelogram is squashed flat, its area is zero, and the matrix is singular, so “determinant zero” and “the columns collapse onto one line” are the same event.

Definition 6.11: Normal equations (matrix form)

The normal equations of least squares are the linear system

X′X b=X′Y,\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}\,\mathbf{b} = \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y},X′Xb=X′Y,

whose solution b\mathbf{b}b is the least-squares coefficient vector. This is the matrix twin of the two scalar normal equations of Chapter 2 (2.2 Least squares from first principles).

Applying the inverse to the normal equations gives the least-squares estimator in one line.

Theorem 6.12: Least-squares solution

If X\mathbf{X}X has full column rank, so that X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X is invertible, then the normal equations X′X b=X′Y\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}\,\mathbf{b} = \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}X′Xb=X′Y have the unique solution

b=(X′X)−1X′Y.\mathbf{b} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}.b=(X′X)−1X′Y.

In words: multiply the stored cross-products X′Y\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}X′Y by the inverse of the cross-product matrix X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X, and out come all ppp coefficient estimates at once. This one formula is the whole of least-squares estimation, for any number of predictors.

R and Python¶

First the 2×22 \times 22×2 inverse formula, checked on a small symmetric matrix, so you can see A−1A=I\mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{I}A−1A=I happen. Both languages invert with a single call: solve in R, np.linalg.inv in Python.

M <- matrix(c(2, 1,
              1, 2), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE)
Minv <- solve(M)
Minv
round(M %*% Minv, 6)
           [,1]       [,2]
[1,]  0.6666667 -0.3333333
[2,] -0.3333333  0.6666667
     [,1] [,2]
[1,]    1    0
[2,]    0    1
M = np.array([[2.0, 1.0],
              [1.0, 2.0]])
Minv = np.linalg.inv(M)
print(Minv)
print(np.round(M @ Minv, 6))
[[ 0.66666667 -0.33333333]
 [-0.33333333  0.66666667]]
[[1. 0.]
 [0. 1.]]

The determinant is 2⋅2−1⋅1=32\cdot 2 - 1 \cdot 1 = 32⋅2−1⋅1=3, so the inverse is 13(2−1−12)\tfrac{1}{3}\left(\begin{smallmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{smallmatrix}\right)31​(2−1​−12​), matching the printout, and M−1M\mathbf{M}^{-1}\mathbf{M}M−1M returns the identity.

You will almost never invert a matrix bigger than this by hand; working the two-by-two case once is enough to see where the answer comes from and watch the identity appear, so a software inverse is never magic. From here on we let solve and np.linalg.inv do the arithmetic, and the next example turns the inverse loose on the full Dwaine fit.

Example 6.3: Solving the normal equations for Dwaine

Question. Invert X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X and compute the coefficient vector b=(X′X)−1X′Y\mathbf{b} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}b=(X′X)−1X′Y. Do the numbers match what lm and statsmodels report?

Intuition. We already stored X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X and X′Y\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}X′Y. Inverting the first and multiplying by the second solves for all three coefficients at once.

Formula. b=(X′X)−1X′Y\mathbf{b} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}b=(X′X)−1X′Y.

Computation.

XtX_inv <- solve(XtX)
round(XtX_inv, 6)
round(XtX %*% XtX_inv, 6)
          intercept  targtpop  dispoinc
intercept 29.728923  0.072183 -1.992553
targtpop   0.072183  0.000370 -0.005550
dispoinc  -1.992553 -0.005550  0.136311
          intercept targtpop dispoinc
intercept         1        0        0
targtpop          0        1        0
dispoinc          0        0        1
b <- XtX_inv %*% XtY
b
               [,1]
intercept -68.85707
targtpop    1.45456
dispoinc    9.36550
fit <- lm(sales ~ targtpop + dispoinc, data = dwaine)
coef(fit)
(Intercept)    targtpop    dispoinc
  -68.85707     1.45456     9.36550

Now the same computation in Python.

XtX_inv = np.linalg.inv(XtX)
print(np.round(XtX_inv, 6))
print(np.round(XtX @ XtX_inv, 6))
[[ 2.9728923e+01  7.2183000e-02 -1.9925530e+00]
 [ 7.2183000e-02  3.7000000e-04 -5.5500000e-03]
 [-1.9925530e+00 -5.5500000e-03  1.3631100e-01]]
[[ 1. -0. -0.]
 [ 0.  1. -0.]
 [-0. -0.  1.]]
b = XtX_inv @ XtY
print(b)
[-68.85707315   1.45455958   9.36550038]
fit = smf.ols("sales ~ targtpop + dispoinc", data=dwaine).fit()
print(fit.params)
Intercept   -68.857073
targtpop      1.454560
dispoinc      9.365500
dtype: float64

Interpretation. The hand-built estimator gives b=(−68.86, 1.4546, 9.3655)′\mathbf{b} = (-68.86,\ 1.4546,\ 9.3655)'b=(−68.86, 1.4546, 9.3655)′, and both lm and statsmodels return the identical numbers, confirming that the built-in routines do exactly this matrix computation. The fitted model is sales^=−68.86+1.4546 (targtpop)+9.3655 (dispoinc)\widehat{\text{sales}} = -68.86 + 1.4546\,(\text{targtpop}) + 9.3655\,(\text{dispoinc})sales=−68.86+1.4546(targtpop)+9.3655(dispoinc). Each extra thousand young residents adds about 1.45 thousand dollars of predicted sales, holding income fixed, and each extra thousand dollars of income adds about 9.37 thousand, holding population fixed. Chapter 8 unpacks that “holding fixed” reading (8.3 Extra sums of squares); here, one matrix inverse produced the entire fit.

Try it 6.4

Using the 2×22 \times 22×2 formula, invert A=(4213)\mathbf{A} = \left(\begin{smallmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 1 & 3 \end{smallmatrix}\right)A=(41​23​) by hand. Then explain why B=(2412)\mathbf{B} = \left(\begin{smallmatrix} 2 & 4 \\ 1 & 2 \end{smallmatrix}\right)B=(21​42​) has no inverse.

Solution

For A\mathbf{A}A, the determinant is 4⋅3−2⋅1=104 \cdot 3 - 2 \cdot 1 = 104⋅3−2⋅1=10, so A−1=110(3−2−14)=(0.3−0.2−0.10.4)\mathbf{A}^{-1} = \tfrac{1}{10}\left(\begin{smallmatrix} 3 & -2 \\ -1 & 4 \end{smallmatrix}\right) = \left(\begin{smallmatrix} 0.3 & -0.2 \\ -0.1 & 0.4 \end{smallmatrix}\right)A−1=101​(3−1​−24​)=(0.3−0.1​−0.20.4​). For B\mathbf{B}B, the determinant is 2⋅2−4⋅1=02 \cdot 2 - 4 \cdot 1 = 02⋅2−4⋅1=0, so no inverse exists. The reason is visible in the columns: column 2 is exactly twice column 1, so the columns are linearly dependent and B\mathbf{B}B is rank deficient.

A determinant of zero is easier to feel than to define, so pull the two columns together yourself and watch the inverse come apart long before they meet.

6.5 Idempotent and projection matrices¶

Intuition¶

The coefficients are done, so the model can now make its own predictions. The question of this section is where those predictions come from and what they look like as a picture. The answer is a single table of numbers that turns the observed sales into the model’s fitted sales in one step, and that step is a shadow: it flattens the data onto the surface the predictors can reach.

With b\mathbf{b}b in hand, the fitted values are Y^=Xb\widehat{\mathbf{Y}} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{b}Y=Xb. Substitute the estimator and something remarkable appears:

Y^=Xb=X(X′X)−1X′Y=HY,H=X(X′X)−1X′.\widehat{\mathbf{Y}} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{b} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{Y}, \qquad \mathbf{H} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'.Y=Xb=X(X′X)−1X′Y=HY,H=X(X′X)−1X′.
Definition 6.13: Hat matrix

The hat matrix is H=X(X′X)−1X′\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'H=X(X′X)−1X′, the single matrix that turns the observed responses Y\mathbf{Y}Y into the fitted values Y^=HY\widehat{\mathbf{Y}} = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{Y}Y=HY: it “puts the hat on Y\mathbf{Y}Y.”

Geometrically the hat matrix is a projection (Definition 6.14). Picture Y\mathbf{Y}Y as an arrow in nnn-dimensional space; the fitted values a regression can produce all lie in a flat subspace, the span of the columns of X\mathbf{X}X. The hat matrix drops Y\mathbf{Y}Y straight down onto that subspace, landing at the closest point, and the residual is the perpendicular leftover. Figure 7 shows this picture, the subject of 7.2 The geometry of least squares.

Two algebraic properties make H\mathbf{H}H a projection, and they are the workhorses of the diagnostics in Chapter 9. First, H\mathbf{H}H is symmetric. Second, H\mathbf{H}H is idempotent: applying it twice is the same as applying it once, HH=H\mathbf{H}\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{H}HH=H. That makes sense for a projection, since once a vector is flattened onto the plane, flattening again does nothing.

The shadow picture is worth holding onto. Your shadow on flat ground keeps your left-right and forward-back position but flattens your height to zero; the hat matrix does the same to Y\mathbf{Y}Y, flattening away the part of the response that no combination of the predictors could reproduce, which is the residual.

These properties are not decoration: Chapter 9 reads the diagonal of H\mathbf{H}H to find which cities pull hardest on the fitted surface, Chapter 7 uses its trace to count degrees of freedom, and the perpendicular picture is what makes least squares the closest fit, with no point in the plane nearer to Y\mathbf{Y}Y than its own shadow.

Key idea

Least squares is a projection. The hat matrix drops the response Y\mathbf{Y}Y straight down onto the space of values the predictors can reproduce, landing at the closest point Y^\widehat{\mathbf{Y}}Y, and the residual is the perpendicular leftover. “Best fit” and “shortest residual” are the same geometric fact.

Formula¶

Definition 6.14: Idempotent and projection matrix

A square matrix P\mathbf{P}P is idempotent if PP=P\mathbf{P}\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{P}PP=P: applying it twice is the same as applying it once. A symmetric idempotent matrix is a projection matrix.

The two projections of regression are

H=X(X′X)−1X′,I−H,\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}', \qquad \mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H},H=X(X′X)−1X′,I−H,

with Y^=HY\widehat{\mathbf{Y}} = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{Y}Y=HY the fitted values and e=Y−Y^=(I−H)Y\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{Y} - \widehat{\mathbf{Y}} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})\mathbf{Y}e=Y−Y=(I−H)Y the residuals. In words: H\mathbf{H}H projects onto the space of fittable values, and I−H\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H}I−H projects onto the leftover space of residuals.

Definition 6.15: Trace

The trace of a square matrix is the sum of its diagonal entries. For the hat matrix, tr⁡(H)=p\operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{H}) = ptr(H)=p, the number of parameters, a fact Chapter 7 uses to explain the n−pn - pn−p divisor for s2s^2s2.

The two defining properties of H\mathbf{H}H are what make it a projection.

Theorem 6.16: The hat matrix is a projection

The hat matrix H=X(X′X)−1X′\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'H=X(X′X)−1X′ is symmetric and idempotent, and so is I−H\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H}I−H. Hence both are projection matrices: H\mathbf{H}H projects onto the column space of X\mathbf{X}X and I−H\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H}I−H onto the residual space.

Proof. Write G=(X′X)−1\mathbf{G} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}G=(X′X)−1, which is symmetric because X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X is (its inverse inherits symmetry: G′=((X′X)−1)′=((X′X)′)−1=(X′X)−1=G\mathbf{G}' = ((\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1})' = ((\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})')^{-1} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1} = \mathbf{G}G′=((X′X)−1)′=((X′X)′)−1=(X′X)−1=G). For symmetry of H\mathbf{H}H, apply the order-reversing transpose rule of 6.2 Transpose and matrix multiplication twice:

H′=(XGX′)′=(X′)′G′X′=XGX′=H.\mathbf{H}' = (\mathbf{X}\mathbf{G}\mathbf{X}')' = (\mathbf{X}')'\mathbf{G}'\mathbf{X}' = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{G}\mathbf{X}' = \mathbf{H}.H′=(XGX′)′=(X′)′G′X′=XGX′=H.

For idempotency, multiply H\mathbf{H}H by itself and cancel the inner X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X against its inverse:

HH=XG(X′X)GX′=XGX′=H,\mathbf{H}\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})\mathbf{G}\mathbf{X}' = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{G}\mathbf{X}' = \mathbf{H},HH=XG(X′X)GX′=XGX′=H,

using (X′X)G=(X′X)(X′X)−1=I(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})\mathbf{G} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1} = \mathbf{I}(X′X)G=(X′X)(X′X)−1=I. The same two steps show I−H\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H}I−H is symmetric and idempotent: (I−H)(I−H)=I−2H+HH=I−2H+H=I−H(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H}) = \mathbf{I} - 2\mathbf{H} + \mathbf{H}\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{I} - 2\mathbf{H} + \mathbf{H} = \mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H}(I−H)(I−H)=I−2H+HH=I−2H+H=I−H. ■\blacksquare■

R and Python¶

Example 6.4: The hat matrix in action

Question. Build H\mathbf{H}H for Dwaine, confirm it is symmetric and idempotent with trace 3, and use it to produce the fitted values and residuals.

Intuition. H\mathbf{H}H is 21×2121 \times 2121×21. We check the two defining properties numerically, then apply H\mathbf{H}H and I−H\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H}I−H to Y\mathbf{Y}Y.

Formula. H=X(X′X)−1X′\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'H=X(X′X)−1X′, Y^=HY\widehat{\mathbf{Y}} = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{Y}Y=HY, e=(I−H)Y\mathbf{e} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})\mathbf{Y}e=(I−H)Y.

Computation.

H <- X %*% XtX_inv %*% t(X)
c(symmetric = isSymmetric(round(H, 10)),
  idempotent = all(abs(H %*% H - H) < 1e-10))
round(sum(diag(H)), 6)
 symmetric idempotent
      TRUE       TRUE
[1] 3
Yhat <- H %*% Y
e <- (diag(n) - H) %*% Y
round(head(cbind(Y, Yhat, e), 3), 3)
         Y
[1,] 174.4 187.184 -12.784
[2,] 164.4 154.229  10.171
[3,] 244.2 234.396   9.804
H = X @ XtX_inv @ X.T
print(np.allclose(H, H.T), np.allclose(H @ H, H))
print(round(np.trace(H), 6))
True True
3.0
Yhat = H @ Y
e = (np.eye(n) - H) @ Y
print(np.round(np.column_stack([Y, Yhat, e])[:3], 3))
[[174.4   187.184 -12.784]
 [164.4   154.229  10.171]
 [244.2   234.396   9.804]]

Interpretation. The hat matrix is symmetric and idempotent, with trace 3=p3 = p3=p, exactly as the derivation promised. Applying it, city 1’s fitted sales are 187.2 against actual 174.4, a residual of -12.8 (the model slightly overpredicts). The diagonal entries of H\mathbf{H}H, not printed here, are the leverage values hiih_{ii}hii​ that Chapter 9 turns into influence diagnostics: a large one marks a city whose response has an outsized say in the fit. Every residual analysis in the book starts from this one matrix.

Try it 6.5

A projection matrix P\mathbf{P}P satisfies PP=P\mathbf{P}\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{P}PP=P. Show that if P\mathbf{P}P is idempotent then so is I−P\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{P}I−P, and compute (I−P)P(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{P})\mathbf{P}(I−P)P.

Solution

Expand (I−P)(I−P)=I−P−P+PP=I−2P+P=I−P(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{P})(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{P}) = \mathbf{I} - \mathbf{P} - \mathbf{P} + \mathbf{P}\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{I} - 2\mathbf{P} + \mathbf{P} = \mathbf{I} - \mathbf{P}(I−P)(I−P)=I−P−P+PP=I−2P+P=I−P, so I−P\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{P}I−P is idempotent. And (I−P)P=P−PP=P−P=0(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{P})\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{P} - \mathbf{P}\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{P} - \mathbf{P} = \mathbf{0}(I−P)P=P−PP=P−P=0. The two projections annihilate each other, which is the algebra behind fitted values and residuals being orthogonal.

The projection is the one idea in this chapter a flat page cannot really show, so turn the picture around and move Y\mathbf{Y}Y with your own finger.

6.6 Quadratic forms and sums of squares¶

Intuition¶

Every “sum of squares” in regression is secretly one matrix expression called a quadratic form (Definition 6.17). The error sum of squares is SSE=∑ei2\mathrm{SSE} = \sum e_i^2SSE=∑ei2​, and a sum of squared entries of a vector is exactly that vector dotted with itself: SSE=e′e\mathrm{SSE} = \mathbf{e}'\mathbf{e}SSE=e′e. Because e=(I−H)Y\mathbf{e} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})\mathbf{Y}e=(I−H)Y, a little algebra rewrites SSE using only Y\mathbf{Y}Y and a projection matrix in the middle. That middle-matrix pattern, Y′AY\mathbf{Y}'\mathbf{A}\mathbf{Y}Y′AY, is a quadratic form, and SSTO, SSR, and SSE are all of this shape.

Why care? Two reasons. First, a quadratic form makes the degrees of freedom and the expected value of each sum of squares fall out of the middle matrix, how Chapter 7 explains the ANOVA table. Second, sums of squares can never be negative, and that is guaranteed by a property of the middle matrix called positive semidefiniteness. Figure 9 shows why a positive-definite form is shaped like a bowl that never dips below zero.

The payoff is that this view survives even when the sum no longer looks like a sum of squares. Written as Y′(I−H)Y\mathbf{Y}'(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})\mathbf{Y}Y′(I−H)Y, SSE does not visibly square anything, yet the middle matrix still guarantees it cannot go negative. The same reasoning applies to a variance, a sum of squares in disguise, so the covariance matrix of the next section can never hold a negative variance.

Formula¶

Definition 6.17: Quadratic form and positive definiteness

A quadratic form in a vector u\mathbf{u}u is a scalar u′Au=∑i∑jAijuiuj\mathbf{u}'\mathbf{A}\mathbf{u} = \sum_i \sum_j A_{ij} u_i u_ju′Au=∑i​∑j​Aij​ui​uj​ for a symmetric matrix A\mathbf{A}A, a weighted sum of all products of pairs of entries. The matrix A\mathbf{A}A is positive semidefinite if u′Au≥0\mathbf{u}'\mathbf{A}\mathbf{u} \ge 0u′Au≥0 for every u\mathbf{u}u, and positive definite if the form is strictly positive for every nonzero u\mathbf{u}u.

The three regression sums of squares are all quadratic forms in Y\mathbf{Y}Y, and their middle matrices carry the ANOVA decomposition.

Theorem 6.18: Sums of squares as quadratic forms

With J\mathbf{J}J the n×nn \times nn×n matrix of all ones,

SSE=Y′(I−H)Y,SSR=Y′ ⁣(H−1nJ) ⁣Y,SSTO=Y′ ⁣(I−1nJ) ⁣Y,\mathrm{SSE} = \mathbf{Y}'(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})\mathbf{Y}, \qquad \mathrm{SSR} = \mathbf{Y}'\!\left(\mathbf{H} - \tfrac{1}{n}\mathbf{J}\right)\!\mathbf{Y}, \qquad \mathrm{SSTO} = \mathbf{Y}'\!\left(\mathbf{I} - \tfrac{1}{n}\mathbf{J}\right)\!\mathbf{Y},SSE=Y′(I−H)Y,SSR=Y′(H−n1​J)Y,SSTO=Y′(I−n1​J)Y,

each with a symmetric, idempotent middle matrix. The middle matrices add, (I−H)+(H−1nJ)=I−1nJ(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H}) + (\mathbf{H} - \tfrac{1}{n}\mathbf{J}) = \mathbf{I} - \tfrac{1}{n}\mathbf{J}(I−H)+(H−n1​J)=I−n1​J, which is the matrix statement of SSTO=SSR+SSE\mathrm{SSTO} = \mathrm{SSR} + \mathrm{SSE}SSTO=SSR+SSE, and SSE≥0\mathrm{SSE} \ge 0SSE≥0 because its middle matrix is positive semidefinite.

Proof (nonnegativity). Because I−H\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H}I−H is symmetric and idempotent, SSE=Y′(I−H)Y=Y′(I−H)′(I−H)Y=e′e≥0\mathrm{SSE} = \mathbf{Y}'(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{Y}'(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})'(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{e}'\mathbf{e} \ge 0SSE=Y′(I−H)Y=Y′(I−H)′(I−H)Y=e′e≥0, so SSE is a genuine sum of squares and can never be negative; the same holds for SSR and SSTO. The additive decomposition of the middle matrices is the matrix statement of the ANOVA identity SSTO=SSR+SSE\mathrm{SSTO} = \mathrm{SSR} + \mathrm{SSE}SSTO=SSR+SSE (3.6 The analysis of variance approach). ■\blacksquare■

In words: the sums of squares are quadratic forms, and the idempotent middle matrices both guarantee nonnegativity and, in Chapter 7, hand us the degrees of freedom through their traces.

R and Python¶

Example 6.5: SSE as a quadratic form

Question. Compute SSE for Dwaine as the quadratic form Y′(I−H)Y\mathbf{Y}'(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})\mathbf{Y}Y′(I−H)Y, and use it to estimate the error variance s2=MSEs^2 = \mathrm{MSE}s2=MSE.

Intuition. We already have e=(I−H)Y\mathbf{e} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})\mathbf{Y}e=(I−H)Y, so SSE=e′e\mathrm{SSE} = \mathbf{e}'\mathbf{e}SSE=e′e is a single number. Dividing by n−pn - pn−p degrees of freedom gives the variance estimate.

Formula. SSE=e′e\mathrm{SSE} = \mathbf{e}'\mathbf{e}SSE=e′e, MSE=SSE/(n−p)\mathrm{MSE} = \mathrm{SSE}/(n - p)MSE=SSE/(n−p) with p=3p = 3p=3.

Computation.

SSE <- as.numeric(t(e) %*% e)
p <- ncol(X)
MSE <- SSE / (n - p)
round(c(SSE = SSE, df = n - p, MSE = MSE), 4)
      SSE        df       MSE
2180.9274   18.0000  121.1626
SSE = float(e.T @ e)
p = X.shape[1]
MSE = SSE / (n - p)
print(round(SSE, 4), n - p, round(MSE, 4))
2180.9274 18 121.1626

Interpretation. The residual quadratic form gives SSE=2180.9\mathrm{SSE} = 2180.9SSE=2180.9 on n−p=21−3=18n - p = 21 - 3 = 18n−p=21−3=18 degrees of freedom, so MSE=121.2\mathrm{MSE} = 121.2MSE=121.2 and the residual standard error is s=121.2≈11.0s = \sqrt{121.2} \approx 11.0s=121.2​≈11.0 thousand dollars: even knowing a city’s young population and income, the sales prediction is typically off by about 11 thousand dollars. The divisor 18 counts the observations left after spending p=3p = 3p=3 on the coefficients, the multiple-regression version of the n−2n - 2n−2 rule from Chapter 2; Chapter 7 reads this n−pn - pn−p off the trace of the hat matrix and uses it to prove MSE is unbiased (7.3 The hat matrix).

Try it 6.6

Let u=(u1,u2)′\mathbf{u} = (u_1, u_2)'u=(u1​,u2​)′ and A=(2003)\mathbf{A} = \left(\begin{smallmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 \end{smallmatrix}\right)A=(20​03​). Write out the quadratic form u′Au\mathbf{u}'\mathbf{A}\mathbf{u}u′Au as an ordinary expression in u1u_1u1​ and u2u_2u2​, and explain why it is positive definite.

Solution

u′Au=2u12+3u22\mathbf{u}'\mathbf{A}\mathbf{u} = 2u_1^2 + 3u_2^2u′Au=2u12​+3u22​. Both coefficients are positive, so the expression is a sum of two nonnegative terms and is zero only when u1=u2=0u_1 = u_2 = 0u1​=u2​=0. Hence u′Au>0\mathbf{u}'\mathbf{A}\mathbf{u} > 0u′Au>0 for every nonzero u\mathbf{u}u, which is the definition of positive definite. Its graph is the upward bowl of Figure 9.

6.7 Random vectors, expectation, and covariance matrices¶

Intuition¶

So far the matrices held fixed numbers. But Y\mathbf{Y}Y is random: rerun the 21 cities under the same conditions and the sales would come out a little different, because of the errors ε\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}ε. To describe a vector of random variables we need two things: a vector of means and a table of variances and covariances.

The vector of means is just the expectation applied entry by entry. The table is the covariance matrix (Definition 6.19): its diagonal holds the variance of each entry, and its off-diagonals hold the covariance between pairs of entries. For the regression errors, this table is simple: constant variance σ2\sigma^2σ2 down the diagonal (every error has the same spread) and zeros off it (distinct errors are uncorrelated), so Cov⁡{ε}=σ2I\operatorname{Cov}\{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\} = \sigma^2\mathbf{I}Cov{ε}=σ2I. A two-line rule for how means and covariances travel through a linear map then yields the covariance matrix of b\mathbf{b}b, the source of every standard error.

Formula¶

Definition 6.19: Random vector, mean vector, and covariance matrix

A random vector W=(W1,…,Wm)′\mathbf{W} = (W_1, \dots, W_m)'W=(W1​,…,Wm​)′ is a vector whose entries are random variables. Its mean vector and covariance matrix are

E{W}=(E{W1}⋮E{Wm}),Cov⁡{W}=(Var⁡{W1}⋯Cov⁡{W1,Wm}⋮⋱⋮Cov⁡{Wm,W1}⋯Var⁡{Wm}),E\{\mathbf{W}\} = \begin{pmatrix} E\{W_1\} \\ \vdots \\ E\{W_m\} \end{pmatrix}, \qquad \operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{W}\} = \begin{pmatrix} \operatorname{Var}\{W_1\} & \cdots & \operatorname{Cov}\{W_1, W_m\} \\ \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ \operatorname{Cov}\{W_m, W_1\} & \cdots & \operatorname{Var}\{W_m\} \end{pmatrix},E{W}=⎝⎛​E{W1​}⋮E{Wm​}​⎠⎞​,Cov{W}=⎝⎛​Var{W1​}⋮Cov{Wm​,W1​}​⋯⋱⋯​Cov{W1​,Wm​}⋮Var{Wm​}​⎠⎞​,

with variances on the diagonal (always nonnegative) and a symmetric off-diagonal because Cov⁡{Wi,Wj}=Cov⁡{Wj,Wi}\operatorname{Cov}\{W_i, W_j\} = \operatorname{Cov}\{W_j, W_i\}Cov{Wi​,Wj​}=Cov{Wj​,Wi​}.

The transformation rules for how these travel through a linear map are the heart of the section.

Theorem 6.20: Linear transformation of mean and covariance

For a random vector W\mathbf{W}W, a fixed matrix A\mathbf{A}A, and a fixed vector c\mathbf{c}c,

E{AW+c}=A E{W}+c,Cov⁡{AW}=ACov⁡{W}A′.E\{\mathbf{A}\mathbf{W} + \mathbf{c}\} = \mathbf{A}\,E\{\mathbf{W}\} + \mathbf{c}, \qquad \operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{A}\mathbf{W}\} = \mathbf{A}\operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{W}\}\mathbf{A}'.E{AW+c}=AE{W}+c,Cov{AW}=ACov{W}A′.

In words: expectation passes straight through a linear map, and a covariance matrix gets sandwiched between A\mathbf{A}A and its transpose. Apply these to the regression model, where E{Y}=XβE\{\mathbf{Y}\} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}E{Y}=Xβ and Cov⁡{Y}=σ2I\operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{Y}\} = \sigma^2\mathbf{I}Cov{Y}=σ2I, and the mean and covariance of b\mathbf{b}b follow.

Theorem 6.21: Mean and covariance of the least-squares estimator

Under the linear model Y=Xβ+ε\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}Y=Xβ+ε with E{ε}=0E\{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\} = \mathbf{0}E{ε}=0 and Cov⁡{ε}=σ2I\operatorname{Cov}\{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\} = \sigma^2\mathbf{I}Cov{ε}=σ2I, the estimator b=(X′X)−1X′Y\mathbf{b} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}b=(X′X)−1X′Y is unbiased with

E{b}=β,Cov⁡{b}=σ2(X′X)−1.E\{\mathbf{b}\} = \boldsymbol{\beta}, \qquad \operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{b}\} = \sigma^2 (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}.E{b}=β,Cov{b}=σ2(X′X)−1.

Proof. The estimator is a linear map of Y\mathbf{Y}Y: with A=(X′X)−1X′\mathbf{A} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'A=(X′X)−1X′, we have b=AY\mathbf{b} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{Y}b=AY. For the mean, use E{Y}=XβE\{\mathbf{Y}\} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}E{Y}=Xβ:

E{b}=A E{Y}=(X′X)−1X′Xβ=β,E\{\mathbf{b}\} = \mathbf{A}\,E\{\mathbf{Y}\} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} = \boldsymbol{\beta},E{b}=AE{Y}=(X′X)−1X′Xβ=β,

so b\mathbf{b}b is unbiased for β\boldsymbol{\beta}β. For the covariance, use Cov⁡{Y}=σ2I\operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{Y}\} = \sigma^2\mathbf{I}Cov{Y}=σ2I and the sandwich rule:

Cov⁡{b}=A (σ2I) A′=σ2(X′X)−1X′ X(X′X)−1=σ2(X′X)−1,\operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{b}\} = \mathbf{A}\,(\sigma^2\mathbf{I})\,\mathbf{A}' = \sigma^2 (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\,\mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1} = \sigma^2 (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1},Cov{b}=A(σ2I)A′=σ2(X′X)−1X′X(X′X)−1=σ2(X′X)−1,

where the middle X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X cancels one inverse. So Cov⁡{b}=σ2(X′X)−1\operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{b}\} = \sigma^2 (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}Cov{b}=σ2(X′X)−1: the same inverse we used to compute b\mathbf{b}b also gives, once scaled by σ2\sigma^2σ2, the variance of every coefficient and the covariance of every pair. ■\blacksquare■

Replacing the unknown σ2\sigma^2σ2 by its estimate MSE gives the estimated covariance matrix s2{b}=MSE (X′X)−1s^2\{\mathbf{b}\} = \mathrm{MSE}\,(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}s2{b}=MSE(X′X)−1, whose diagonal square roots are the standard errors s{bk}s\{b_k\}s{bk​} that software prints.

Key idea

The single matrix (X′X)−1(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}(X′X)−1 does two jobs. Multiplied by X′Y\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}X′Y it produces the coefficient estimates; scaled by σ2\sigma^2σ2 it produces their entire covariance matrix, standard errors and correlations alike. Solve the fit once and the precision of every coefficient comes along for free.

R and Python¶

Example 6.6: Standard errors from the covariance matrix

Question. Form the estimated covariance matrix s2{b}=MSE (X′X)−1s^2\{\mathbf{b}\} = \mathrm{MSE}\,(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}s2{b}=MSE(X′X)−1 for Dwaine, read off the coefficient standard errors, and check them against the software summary.

Intuition. Scale the stored inverse by MSE; the diagonal holds the three coefficient variances, and their square roots are the standard errors.

Formula. s2{b}=MSE (X′X)−1s^2\{\mathbf{b}\} = \mathrm{MSE}\,(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}s2{b}=MSE(X′X)−1, and s{bk}=[s2{b}]kks\{b_k\} = \sqrt{[s^2\{\mathbf{b}\}]_{kk}}s{bk​}=[s2{b}]kk​​.

Computation.

cov_b <- MSE * XtX_inv
round(cov_b, 4)
round(sqrt(diag(cov_b)), 4)
          intercept targtpop  dispoinc
intercept 3602.0347   8.7459 -241.4230
targtpop     8.7459   0.0449   -0.6724
dispoinc  -241.4230  -0.6724   16.5158
intercept  targtpop  dispoinc
  60.0170    0.2118    4.0640
round(summary(fit)$coefficients[, 1:2], 4)
            Estimate Std. Error
(Intercept) -68.8571    60.0170
targtpop      1.4546     0.2118
dispoinc      9.3655     4.0640
cov_b = MSE * XtX_inv
print(np.round(cov_b, 4))
print(np.round(np.sqrt(np.diag(cov_b)), 4))
[[ 3.6020347e+03  8.7459000e+00 -2.4142300e+02]
 [ 8.7459000e+00  4.4900000e-02 -6.7240000e-01]
 [-2.4142300e+02 -6.7240000e-01  1.6515800e+01]]
[60.017   0.2118  4.064 ]
print(fit.summary2().tables[1].iloc[:, 0:2].round(4))
             Coef.  Std.Err.
Intercept -68.8571   60.0170
targtpop    1.4546    0.2118
dispoinc    9.3655    4.0640

Interpretation. The diagonal of s2{b}s^2\{\mathbf{b}\}s2{b} gives variances 3602.0, 0.0449, and 16.52, whose square roots are the standard errors 60.02, 0.2118, and 4.064, matching both software summaries to every printed digit. The off-diagonal -0.6724 between the two slopes says that over repeated samples an overestimate of one tends to come with an underestimate of the other, the consequence of the two predictors sharing information. Chapter 7 uses this full matrix, diagonal and off-diagonal alike, to build every confidence interval and joint test.

Try it 6.7

A random vector W\mathbf{W}W has covariance matrix Cov⁡{W}=(4119)\operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{W}\} = \left(\begin{smallmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 1 & 9 \end{smallmatrix}\right)Cov{W}=(41​19​). Let A=(1,1)\mathbf{A} = (1, 1)A=(1,1), a 1×21 \times 21×2 row, so AW=W1+W2\mathbf{A}\mathbf{W} = W_1 + W_2AW=W1​+W2​. Use the covariance rule to find Var⁡{W1+W2}\operatorname{Var}\{W_1 + W_2\}Var{W1​+W2​}.

Solution

By the rule, Var⁡{AW}=ACov⁡{W}A′\operatorname{Var}\{\mathbf{A}\mathbf{W}\} = \mathbf{A}\operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{W}\}\mathbf{A}'Var{AW}=ACov{W}A′. Compute ACov⁡{W}=(1,1)(4119)=(5,10)\mathbf{A}\operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{W}\} = (1,1)\left(\begin{smallmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 1 & 9 \end{smallmatrix}\right) = (5, 10)ACov{W}=(1,1)(41​19​)=(5,10), then (5,10)(1,1)′=15(5, 10)(1, 1)' = 15(5,10)(1,1)′=15. So Var⁡{W1+W2}=15\operatorname{Var}\{W_1 + W_2\} = 15Var{W1​+W2​}=15. This matches the scalar rule Var⁡{W1}+Var⁡{W2}+2Cov⁡{W1,W2}=4+9+2(1)=15\operatorname{Var}\{W_1\} + \operatorname{Var}\{W_2\} + 2\operatorname{Cov}\{W_1, W_2\} = 4 + 9 + 2(1) = 15Var{W1​}+Var{W2​}+2Cov{W1​,W2​}=4+9+2(1)=15.

Durable skill: One object, many disguises

The quantity SSE appeared first as a plain sum ∑ei2\sum e_i^2∑ei2​, then as a dot product e′e\mathbf{e}'\mathbf{e}e′e, then as a quadratic form Y′(I−H)Y\mathbf{Y}'(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})\mathbf{Y}Y′(I−H)Y. All three are the same number, written in the language that makes the next step easiest: the sum for hand computation, the dot product for code, the quadratic form for proving facts about its mean and degrees of freedom. Learning to recognize one object under its different notations is what lets you read advanced statistics. When a new formula looks unfamiliar, ask whether it is an old friend in matrix clothing.

6.8 The multivariate normal, in brief¶

Intuition¶

The mean vector and covariance matrix describe a random vector’s center and spread, but not its full shape. For inference we add one assumption, the same one that turned Chapter 2’s estimates into ttt tests: the errors are normal. Stacked into a vector, normal errors with constant variance and no correlation follow a multivariate normal distribution (Definition 6.22), the several-variable generalization of the bell curve. Its contours are ellipses whose tilt and stretch are read directly off the covariance matrix, as in Figure 10.

What the full distribution adds is how the probability thins out as you move from the center: the same bell-curve falloff you know from one variable, measured along the tilted axes of the covariance ellipse. That is what lets us compute the chance that a coefficient lands within a stated distance of the truth, exactly what a confidence interval reports in the next chapter.

Why this matters: under normal errors the response is multivariate normal, and because b\mathbf{b}b is a linear map of Y\mathbf{Y}Y, so is b\mathbf{b}b. That fact, b∼N(β,σ2(X′X)−1)\mathbf{b} \sim N(\boldsymbol{\beta}, \sigma^2(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1})b∼N(β,σ2(X′X)−1), is the foundation of every confidence interval, ttt test, and FFF test ahead.

One caution keeps this honest. Multivariate normality of b\mathbf{b}b is a consequence of assuming normal errors, not a fact the data hand you for free. If the errors are badly non-normal and the sample is small, that law is only approximate, one reason Chapter 5’s permutation and bootstrap methods exist (5.4 The bootstrap for regression). With a large sample, an averaging effect pulls b\mathbf{b}b toward normal even when the errors are not.

Formula¶

Definition 6.22: Multivariate normal

A random vector W\mathbf{W}W is multivariate normal, written W∼N(μ,Σ)\mathbf{W} \sim N(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma})W∼N(μ,Σ), if it has mean vector μ\boldsymbol{\mu}μ, covariance matrix Σ\boldsymbol{\Sigma}Σ, and a joint density whose exponent is the quadratic form −12(w−μ)′Σ−1(w−μ)-\tfrac{1}{2}(\mathbf{w} - \boldsymbol{\mu})'\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}(\mathbf{w} - \boldsymbol{\mu})−21​(w−μ)′Σ−1(w−μ). Its defining property: any linear map of a multivariate normal vector is again multivariate normal, with mean and covariance given by the transformation rules of Theorem 6.20.

Applying that property to regression under the normal error model gives the sampling law that drives every later inference.

Theorem 6.23: Sampling distribution of b\mathbf{b}b

Under the normal error model ε∼N(0,σ2I)\boldsymbol{\varepsilon} \sim N(\mathbf{0}, \sigma^2\mathbf{I})ε∼N(0,σ2I), the response and the least-squares estimator are multivariate normal:

Y∼N(Xβ, σ2I),b∼N ⁣(β, σ2(X′X)−1).\mathbf{Y} \sim N(\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta},\ \sigma^2\mathbf{I}), \qquad \mathbf{b} \sim N\!\left(\boldsymbol{\beta},\ \sigma^2(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\right).Y∼N(Xβ, σ2I),b∼N(β, σ2(X′X)−1).

The response is centered at the regression surface Xβ\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}Xβ with independent, equal-variance coordinates, and the estimator b\mathbf{b}b is centered at the truth β\boldsymbol{\beta}β (unbiased) with the covariance matrix we derived.

In words: normal errors make both the data and the estimates normal, so every coefficient is normally distributed with a variance we can read from (X′X)−1(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}(X′X)−1, and that is what makes exact inference possible.

R and Python¶

We can demonstrate the covariance-recovery idea by simulation: draw many vectors with a target covariance and confirm the sample covariance matches. Starting from independent standard normals Z\mathbf{Z}Z, the transformation V=ZL′\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{Z}\mathbf{L}'V=ZL′, where L\mathbf{L}L is a matrix square root of the target Σ\boldsymbol{\Sigma}Σ (the Cholesky factor), produces vectors with covariance Σ\boldsymbol{\Sigma}Σ, exactly the sandwich rule at work.

set.seed(4210)
mu <- c(0, 0)
Sigma <- matrix(c(1, 0.8,
                  0.8, 1), nrow = 2)
L <- chol(Sigma)
Z <- matrix(rnorm(2 * 5000), ncol = 2)
V <- Z %*% L
round(cov(V), 3)
      [,1]  [,2]
[1,] 0.967 0.760
[2,] 0.760 0.955
rng = np.random.default_rng(4210)
Sigma = np.array([[1.0, 0.8],
                  [0.8, 1.0]])
L = np.linalg.cholesky(Sigma)
Z = rng.standard_normal((5000, 2))
V = Z @ L.T
print(np.round(np.cov(V, rowvar=False), 3))
[[1.017 0.822]
 [0.822 1.026]]

The sample covariance of the 5000 simulated vectors comes out close to the target (10.80.81)\left(\begin{smallmatrix} 1 & 0.8 \\ 0.8 & 1 \end{smallmatrix}\right)(10.8​0.81​) in both languages, differing only by sampling noise.

The Cholesky factor can stay a black box for now; the point is that a covariance can be planted on purpose, by passing uncorrelated noise through a fixed matrix and letting the sandwich rule do the rest. The covariance of b\mathbf{b}b is the same story: error noise σ2I\sigma^2\mathbf{I}σ2I passed through the fixed matrix (X′X)−1X′(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'(X′X)−1X′, so the simulation and the regression are two uses of one mechanism.

Try it 6.8

Under the normal error model, b∼N(β,σ2(X′X)−1)\mathbf{b} \sim N(\boldsymbol{\beta}, \sigma^2(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1})b∼N(β,σ2(X′X)−1). For the Dwaine fit, which single stored matrix, once scaled, gives the variance of the dispoinc slope? Where is that variance in the printout of Example 6.6?

Solution

The covariance matrix of b\mathbf{b}b is σ2(X′X)−1\sigma^2(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}σ2(X′X)−1, estimated by MSE (X′X)−1\mathrm{MSE}\,(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}MSE(X′X)−1. The variance of the dispoinc slope is its diagonal entry, the bottom-right of the estimated covariance matrix, 16.52 in Example 6.6. Its square root, 16.52=4.064\sqrt{16.52} = 4.06416.52​=4.064, is the standard error reported for dispoinc.

6.9 Chapter summary¶

You can now speak the matrix language the rest of the book is written in: lay a dataset out as a response vector Y\mathbf{Y}Y and a design matrix X\mathbf{X}X, build the products X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X and X′Y\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}X′Y every fit runs on, tell when a matrix is invertible, solve the normal equations for b=(X′X)−1X′Y\mathbf{b} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}b=(X′X)−1X′Y, build the hat matrix and prove it is a projection, express the sums of squares as quadratic forms, and find the mean and covariance of a random vector. For the real Dwaine data, all of this was computed in both R and numpy: b=(−68.86, 1.4546, 9.3655)′\mathbf{b} = (-68.86,\ 1.4546,\ 9.3655)'b=(−68.86, 1.4546, 9.3655)′, SSE=2180.9\mathrm{SSE} = 2180.9SSE=2180.9 on 18 degrees of freedom, MSE=121.2\mathrm{MSE} = 121.2MSE=121.2, and standard errors (60.0, 0.212, 4.06)(60.0,\ 0.212,\ 4.06)(60.0, 0.212, 4.06). From these tools came the two facts that drive all of multiple regression: b\mathbf{b}b is unbiased, and its covariance is σ2(X′X)−1\sigma^2(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}σ2(X′X)−1.

Every piece fits into one pipeline, drawn in Figure 11: from the data you form two summaries, invert one of them, and everything else, the coefficients, the hat matrix, the residuals, and the standard errors, drops out in a fixed order. If you remember the shape of that pipeline, you can rebuild any single formula in it.

Key results at a glance.

ResultStatement or formulaValid when
Transpose of a product (Theorem 6.5)(AB)′=B′A′(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{B})' = \mathbf{B}'\mathbf{A}'(AB)′=B′A′any conformable A,B\mathbf{A}, \mathbf{B}A,B
Invertibility criterion (Theorem 6.9)X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X invertible ⇔X\Leftrightarrow \mathbf{X}⇔X has full column rankX′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X square
Least-squares solution (Theorem 6.12)b=(X′X)−1X′Y\mathbf{b} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}b=(X′X)−1X′YX\mathbf{X}X full column rank
Hat matrix is a projection (Theorem 6.16)H=X(X′X)−1X′\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'H=X(X′X)−1X′ symmetric, idempotent, tr⁡H=p\operatorname{tr}\mathbf{H} = ptrH=pX\mathbf{X}X full column rank
Sums of squares as quadratic forms (Theorem 6.18)SSE=Y′(I−H)Y\mathrm{SSE} = \mathbf{Y}'(\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{H})\mathbf{Y}SSE=Y′(I−H)Y; SSTO=SSR+SSE\mathrm{SSTO} = \mathrm{SSR} + \mathrm{SSE}SSTO=SSR+SSE; SSE≥0\mathrm{SSE} \ge 0SSE≥0any response Y\mathbf{Y}Y
Linear transformation rules (Theorem 6.20)E{AW+c}=AE{W}+cE\{\mathbf{A}\mathbf{W}+\mathbf{c}\} = \mathbf{A}E\{\mathbf{W}\}+\mathbf{c}E{AW+c}=AE{W}+c; Cov⁡{AW}=ACov⁡{W}A′\operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{A}\mathbf{W}\} = \mathbf{A}\operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{W}\}\mathbf{A}'Cov{AW}=ACov{W}A′A,c\mathbf{A}, \mathbf{c}A,c fixed
Mean and covariance of b\mathbf{b}b (Theorem 6.21)E{b}=βE\{\mathbf{b}\} = \boldsymbol{\beta}E{b}=β; Cov⁡{b}=σ2(X′X)−1\operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{b}\} = \sigma^2(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}Cov{b}=σ2(X′X)−1E{ε}=0E\{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\} = \mathbf{0}E{ε}=0, Cov⁡{ε}=σ2I\operatorname{Cov}\{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\} = \sigma^2\mathbf{I}Cov{ε}=σ2I
Sampling distribution of b\mathbf{b}b (Theorem 6.23)b∼N(β, σ2(X′X)−1)\mathbf{b} \sim N(\boldsymbol{\beta},\ \sigma^2(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1})b∼N(β, σ2(X′X)−1)normal errors ε∼N(0,σ2I)\boldsymbol{\varepsilon} \sim N(\mathbf{0}, \sigma^2\mathbf{I})ε∼N(0,σ2I)

Key terms. matrix, vector, design matrix, transpose, matrix multiplication, conformable, identity matrix, symmetric matrix, linear independence, rank, determinant, inverse, normal equations, hat matrix, idempotent matrix, projection matrix, trace, quadratic form, positive definite, random vector, covariance matrix, multivariate normal.

You should now be able to.

  • Represent a regression dataset as a response vector Y\mathbf{Y}Y and a design matrix X\mathbf{X}X, and write the model as Y=Xβ+ε\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}Y=Xβ+ε.

  • Compute transposes and matrix products, and build X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X and X′Y\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}X′Y by hand and with software.

  • Decide when a square matrix has an inverse using rank and the determinant, and explain what perfect collinearity does to X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X.

  • Solve the normal equations for b=(X′X)−1X′Y\mathbf{b} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}b=(X′X)−1X′Y.

  • Verify that the hat matrix H\mathbf{H}H is symmetric and idempotent, and explain why fitted values and residuals are projections.

  • Express SSE, SSR, and SSTO as quadratic forms, and connect positive definiteness to nonnegative sums of squares.

  • Derive the mean vector and covariance matrix of a random vector, and use them to show Cov⁡{b}=σ2(X′X)−1\operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{b}\} = \sigma^2(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}Cov{b}=σ2(X′X)−1.

  • State the multivariate normal model for Y\mathbf{Y}Y and b\mathbf{b}b and describe how it powers the inference of later chapters.

Where this fits. This chapter is the toolbox that makes the FIT and USE stages of the course workflow (The modeling workflow) work for more than one predictor: the estimator b=(X′X)−1X′Y\mathbf{b} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}b=(X′X)−1X′Y fits every multiple regression, and the covariance matrix σ2(X′X)−1\sigma^2(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}σ2(X′X)−1 is how every coefficient is later tested. Chapter 2 built these ideas for one predictor with scalar algebra (2.2 Least squares from first principles); we have now generalized them to any number. Next, Chapter 7 takes the same Dwaine X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X you computed and proves why these formulas are the right ones: it derives least squares two ways (7.1 The model and least squares in matrix form), reads the degrees of freedom off the hat matrix (7.3 The hat matrix), and proves the Gauss-Markov theorem (7.6 The Gauss-Markov theorem). It is the most abstract chapter in the course, and it is built almost entirely from the matrix facts you assembled here, so the work of this chapter is exactly what makes the next one readable.

6.10 Frequently asked questions¶

Q1. Why do we glue a column of ones onto X\mathbf{X}X? So the intercept can be treated as just another coefficient. Multiplying the ones column by β0\beta_0β0​ gives β0\beta_0β0​ in every row, which is exactly what an intercept does. Without the ones column, the formula Xβ\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}Xβ would have no constant term, and you would be forcing the regression surface through the origin.

Q2. Is X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X the same as squaring X\mathbf{X}X? No. X\mathbf{X}X is usually not square, so X2=XX\mathbf{X}^2 = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{X}X2=XX is not even defined. X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X multiplies the transpose (p×np \times np×n) by X\mathbf{X}X (n×pn \times pn×p) to make a p×pp \times pp×p matrix of sums of products. It is the closest matrix analogue of “sum of squares,” which is why it sits at the center of least squares.

Q3. What actually goes wrong when X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X is singular? Two predictor columns carry the same information, so the data cannot decide how to split an effect between them. Infinitely many coefficient vectors fit equally well, the inverse does not exist, and software either errors out or silently drops a predictor. This is the extreme end of the multicollinearity you meet in 12.1 Multicollinearity and the variance inflation factor.

Q4. Do I have to invert X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X by hand? No. Past 2×22 \times 22×2, let solve or np.linalg.inv do it, and in real work prefer the routines inside lm and statsmodels, which solve the normal equations without forming the inverse. Computing the inverse here just shows that the software does exactly the matrix algebra of this chapter, so it is never a black box.

Q5. Why is the hat matrix called a projection? Because it takes the response vector Y\mathbf{Y}Y and drops it perpendicularly onto the flat subspace of all fittable values (the column space of X\mathbf{X}X), landing at the closest point, Y^\widehat{\mathbf{Y}}Y. Projecting a second time changes nothing, which is the algebraic property HH=H\mathbf{H}\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{H}HH=H. The residual is the part of Y\mathbf{Y}Y left sticking out, at a right angle to the subspace.

Q6. Where does the normal distribution enter? Only in 6.8 The multivariate normal, in brief, and only for inference. Building X\mathbf{X}X, computing b\mathbf{b}b, the hat matrix, SSE, and the covariance matrix all use no distributional assumption beyond mean zero, constant variance, and uncorrelated errors. Normality is added on top so that b\mathbf{b}b is multivariate normal and we can build exact ttt and FFF procedures, which is Chapter 7’s job.

Q7. Do I have to memorize all these identities, and should the negative entries in the covariance matrix of b\mathbf{b}b worry me? No on both counts. Hold on to three things: the model Y=Xβ+ε\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}Y=Xβ+ε, the estimator b=(X′X)−1X′Y\mathbf{b} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}b=(X′X)−1X′Y, and the covariance σ2(X′X)−1\sigma^2(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}σ2(X′X)−1; everything else builds one of those or reads a number out of it, so the identities become things you rederive rather than recall. As for the negative entries, only the off-diagonal covariances can go negative (the diagonal variances never do), and a negative one between two slopes just means that across repeated samples overestimating one tends to accompany underestimating the other. It is information, not an error.

6.11 Practice problems¶

Note

Unless a problem says otherwise, use the dwaine.csv dataset, the design matrix X\mathbf{X}X with columns (ones, targtpop, dispoinc), and the fitted model with b=(−68.86, 1.4546, 9.3655)′\mathbf{b} = (-68.86,\ 1.4546,\ 9.3655)'b=(−68.86, 1.4546, 9.3655)′, MSE=121.2\mathrm{MSE} = 121.2MSE=121.2, n=21n = 21n=21, p=3p = 3p=3. Problems are marked (A) concepts, (B) theory, or (C) data analysis. Odd-numbered answers appear in Appendix H; full solutions are in the instructor materials.

  1. (A) Give the dimensions of X\mathbf{X}X, X′\mathbf{X}'X′, X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X, X′Y\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}X′Y, b\mathbf{b}b, and H\mathbf{H}H for the Dwaine model, and say in one phrase what each represents.

  2. (A) Explain why the first column of the design matrix is all ones, and what would change in the model if it were removed.

  3. (A) State the rule for when two matrices can be multiplied, and use it to explain why XX\mathbf{X}\mathbf{X}XX is undefined for the 21×321 \times 321×3 Dwaine design matrix but X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X is defined.

  4. (A) In words, what is the entry in row 1, column 1 of X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X equal to, and why? What are the other entries of the first row?

  5. (A) Define an idempotent matrix and a symmetric matrix, and state which of these properties the hat matrix H\mathbf{H}H has.

  6. (A) A colleague writes Xb=Y\mathbf{X}\mathbf{b} = \mathbf{Y}Xb=Y and “cancels X\mathbf{X}X” from X′Xb=X′Y\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}\mathbf{b} = \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}X′Xb=X′Y. Explain the two things wrong with this.

  7. (A) Explain what it means for the columns of X\mathbf{X}X to be linearly dependent, and what that does to X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X and to the least-squares fit.

  8. (A) The covariance matrix of b\mathbf{b}b has a negative off-diagonal entry between the two slopes. Interpret its sign in one sentence.

  9. (B) Prove that X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X is symmetric for any matrix X\mathbf{X}X, citing the transpose-of-a-product rule (Theorem 6.5).

  10. (B) Prove the transpose-of-a-product rule (Theorem 6.5), (AB)′=B′A′(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{B})' = \mathbf{B}'\mathbf{A}'(AB)′=B′A′, by comparing the (i,j)(i,j)(i,j) entries of both sides.

  11. (B) Starting from the matrix normal equations X′X b=X′Y\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}\,\mathbf{b} = \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}X′Xb=X′Y, derive b=(X′X)−1X′Y\mathbf{b} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}b=(X′X)−1X′Y (Theorem 6.12), stating the condition on X\mathbf{X}X that the step requires.

  12. (B) Prove that the hat matrix H=X(X′X)−1X′\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'H=X(X′X)−1X′ is symmetric and idempotent (Theorem 6.16). Then show I−H\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H}I−H is idempotent.

  13. (B) Show that HX=X\mathbf{H}\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{X}HX=X, and explain geometrically why projecting each column of X\mathbf{X}X onto the column space of X\mathbf{X}X leaves it unchanged.

  14. (B) Using e=(I−H)Y\mathbf{e} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})\mathbf{Y}e=(I−H)Y and Y^=HY\widehat{\mathbf{Y}} = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{Y}Y=HY, prove that X′e=0\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{0}X′e=0 and that Y^′e=0\widehat{\mathbf{Y}}'\mathbf{e} = 0Y′e=0 (fitted values are orthogonal to residuals).

  15. (B) Prove the covariance transformation rule Cov⁡{AW}=ACov⁡{W}A′\operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{A}\mathbf{W}\} = \mathbf{A}\operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{W}\}\mathbf{A}'Cov{AW}=ACov{W}A′ (part of Theorem 6.20) for a fixed matrix A\mathbf{A}A, starting from the definition Cov⁡{W}=E{(W−μ)(W−μ)′}\operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{W}\} = E\{(\mathbf{W} - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\mathbf{W} - \boldsymbol{\mu})'\}Cov{W}=E{(W−μ)(W−μ)′}.

  16. (B) Use the rules of problem 15 and E{Y}=XβE\{\mathbf{Y}\} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}E{Y}=Xβ, Cov⁡{Y}=σ2I\operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{Y}\} = \sigma^2\mathbf{I}Cov{Y}=σ2I to derive E{b}=βE\{\mathbf{b}\} = \boldsymbol{\beta}E{b}=β and Cov⁡{b}=σ2(X′X)−1\operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{b}\} = \sigma^2(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}Cov{b}=σ2(X′X)−1 (Theorem 6.21).

  17. (B) Show that SSE=Y′(I−H)Y\mathrm{SSE} = \mathbf{Y}'(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})\mathbf{Y}SSE=Y′(I−H)Y equals e′e\mathbf{e}'\mathbf{e}e′e (Theorem 6.18), using the symmetry and idempotency of I−H\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H}I−H, and explain why this proves SSE≥0\mathrm{SSE} \ge 0SSE≥0.

  18. (B) For the 2×22 \times 22×2 matrix A=(abbd)\mathbf{A} = \left(\begin{smallmatrix} a & b \\ b & d \end{smallmatrix}\right)A=(ab​bd​), write the quadratic form u′Au\mathbf{u}'\mathbf{A}\mathbf{u}u′Au in terms of u1,u2u_1, u_2u1​,u2​, and give a condition on a,b,da, b, da,b,d that makes it positive definite.

  19. (B) The fitted values satisfy Y^=HY\widehat{\mathbf{Y}} = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{Y}Y=HY. Prove that Cov⁡{Y^}=σ2H\operatorname{Cov}\{\widehat{\mathbf{Y}}\} = \sigma^2\mathbf{H}Cov{Y}=σ2H and Cov⁡{e}=σ2(I−H)\operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{e}\} = \sigma^2(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})Cov{e}=σ2(I−H), using the covariance rule and the properties of H\mathbf{H}H.
